content.text: Reliability and availability non-functional requirements (NFRs) define the operational resilience standards that an agentic AI system must meet in production. Reliability describes how consistently the system produces correct results without failure; availability describes what fraction of time the system is operational and able to serve requests. These two dimensions are related but distinct: a system can be highly available (rarely down) while being unreliable (producing incorrect outputs), or highly reliable when running but frequently unavailable.

In agentic AI systems, reliability takes on additional dimensions compared to conventional software. A traditional service is reliable if it responds with the correct HTTP status code and body. An agentic system is reliable if it: (1) responds without crashing, (2) completes its reasoning loop without infinite recursion, (3) returns outputs that are factually grounded, and (4) respects tool-call safety boundaries even under adversarial prompts. The functional correctness dimension of reliability interacts with the operational dimension in ways that require specialized NFR definitions.

The standard framework for operationalising these requirements uses three linked concepts: SLA targets (the external commitment to customers), error budgets (the quantified tolerance for failure that follows from the SLA), and MTTR/MTBF targets (the internal engineering targets that determine whether the error budget can be met).

Agentic AI systems often operate as decision-support or automation infrastructure for business processes. A customer service agent that is unavailable for 4 hours during peak hours causes measurable revenue loss. A document processing agent that fails silently—returning plausible-sounding but incorrect outputs—is in some ways worse than one that returns an error, because the failure propagates invisibly into downstream business decisions.

Error budgets provide the mechanism that balances feature velocity against reliability investment. Without an explicit error budget, engineering teams have no principled basis for deciding when to slow down feature development to focus on reliability improvements. The error budget converts an abstract SLA percentage into a concrete monthly allowance of failure time, making the trade-off legible: if 40% of this month's error budget has already been consumed by two incidents, deploying a risky new feature that could trigger another incident is a visible, quantified risk.

MTTR and MTBF targets translate the SLA commitment into engineering design constraints. A 99.9% SLA allows 43.8 minutes of downtime per month. If the team's current mean MTTR is 60 minutes, even a single incident per month will breach the SLA. The MTTR target drives investment in detection speed (alerting latency), diagnosis tooling (distributed tracing, runbooks), and automated recovery mechanisms (circuit breakers, self-healing deployments).
An error budget translates 99.9% availability into 43.8 minutes per month of allowed downtime, making the SLA concrete and actionable for engineering teams.
When error budget is running low, the team has a data-driven reason to freeze risky deployments. When budget is ample, they can ship faster.
MTTR and MTBF targets provide clear criteria for distinguishing a P1 incident (threatens SLA this month) from a P3 (well within budget). Prioritisation becomes objective rather than political.
Reliability NFRs provide the acceptance criteria for chaos experiments: inject a model API outage and verify that MTTR stays below target. Without targets, chaos engineering has no success criterion.

Circuit breaker open/close thresholds can be derived directly from error rate NFRs: if the NFR specifies an error rate below 0.1%, the circuit breaker should open at 5% error rate to protect the system before the SLA is breached.
MTTR measurement gives every incident a quantitative summary (how long did recovery take vs. the target?) that drives concrete improvements in runbooks and detection tooling.

Higher availability targets are exponentially more expensive. The engineering investment required to move from 99.9% to 99.99% is roughly 10 times greater, requiring active-active redundancy, automated failover, zero-downtime deployments, and continuous chaos testing. The investment to reach 99.999% (five nines) is another order of magnitude and typically requires geographic redundancy with sub-second failover.

Reliability NFRs for AI systems must address the non-determinism of LLM outputs. Defining a reliability requirement as 'the system returns a correct answer 99.9% of the time' requires a definition of correctness that is expensive to evaluate. Most agentic AI reliability NFRs focus on operational reliability (the system responds and completes) rather than semantic correctness (the response is factually accurate), deferring the latter to separate evaluation frameworks.

A circuit breaker threshold calibrated too aggressively may open during brief, recoverable spikes, artificially increasing perceived error rates. Calibration requires careful analysis of historical error patterns.

Injecting faults into a production agentic system risks cascading failures if the experiment is not carefully scoped. Chaos engineering for AI systems requires staged rollout: staging environment first, then production with a small traffic percentage.

MTTR can only be measured if the incident start time is precisely defined. Agentic system degradation often begins gradually (elevated error rates, increased latency) rather than with a clean on/off transition, making start-time attribution imprecise.
Availability is expressed as the fraction of time a system is operational:
The three standard enterprise tiers and their allowed annual downtime:
The error budget for a given calendar period is:
For a 99.9% monthly SLA with a 30-day month:
minutes. Every minute of downtime burns this budget.

The burn rate is the rate at which the error budget is being consumed relative to the normal rate. A burn rate of 1.0 means the budget will be exactly exhausted at the end of the measurement period. A burn rate of 2.0 means the budget will be exhausted in half the period:

For a 99.9% SLA (error budget fraction 0.001), an observed error rate of 0.005 yields a burn rate of 5.0—the budget will be consumed in 20% of the period if the error rate persists. Multi-window burn rate alerts (fast window + slow window) catch both sudden spikes and slow sustained degradation.
MTTR breaks into four components: detection time, diagnosis time, mitigation time, and resolution time. Each must be bounded independently:

For a 99.9% SLA with a 43-minute monthly budget, MTTR targets might be: detection within 3 minutes (automated alerting), diagnosis within 10 minutes (tracing + runbook), mitigation within 20 minutes (rollback or circuit breaker), resolution within 60 minutes (root cause fix). The mitigation target—not the resolution target—is what determines whether the SLA is met, because mitigation stops the error budget burn.

attrs.alt: Reliability NFR architecture: SLA Target flows into three parallel tracks—Error Budget (burns on failure), MTTR Target (mean time to recovery), and MTBF Target (mean time between failures). Each track feeds into Circuit Breaker, Chaos Engineering, and Budget Alerting respectively, all converging on Reliability Operations (runbooks, postmortems, game days).

content.text: The architecture links the SLA target to three operational tracks. The
track quantifies the allowable failure time and connects to circuit breaker logic that opens when the error rate threatens the budget. The
track drives chaos engineering experiments that validate whether the team can recover within the target time. The
feeds into burn rate alerting that warns operators when the pace of failures is unsustainable relative to the SLA. All three tracks converge on
: the runbooks, postmortem processes, and game day exercises that embed reliability discipline in the team.

Formal reliability and availability NFRs are required for any agentic system that: has an external customer-facing SLA, is part of a business-critical workflow (order processing, patient data retrieval, financial transactions), or serves as a dependency for other systems that have their own SLAs.
The availability tier (99.9% vs 99.99%) determines architectural requirements (active-passive vs active-active) and should be set before infrastructure decisions are made.
Use three-nine SLAs for most agentic applications.
99.9% (43 min/month) is achievable with standard deployment practices. Four-nine SLAs require significantly more engineering investment and are appropriate only for business-critical applications.
Define separate SLAs for different endpoints.
A streaming inference endpoint may have a 99.9% SLA while a batch processing endpoint has a 99.5% SLA. Composite SLAs obscure the different reliability requirements of different system components.
Revisit SLAs when the underlying model changes.
Model provider SLAs affect the achievable SLA for the agentic system. A system targeting 99.99% availability cannot achieve it if the model provider guarantees only 99.9%.
Define the SLA tier and translate it to an error budget. Document the monthly budget in minutes and the annual budget in hours.
Implement availability measurement. Count successful responses divided by total requests in a rolling window. Define what constitutes a successful response for each endpoint.

Configure burn rate alerts at two windows: a fast window (5 minutes) for high burn rates (> 14x) and a slow window (1 hour) for moderate burn rates (> 2x). Page immediately on fast-window alerts; create tickets for slow-window alerts.

Implement circuit breakers for all LLM API calls and external tool dependencies. Configure the open threshold based on the error rate that would exhaust the error budget within the measurement window.

Write runbooks for each known failure mode: LLM API timeout, model provider rate limit, vector database connection failure, tool API 5xx. Each runbook must include a mitigation step executable in under 10 minutes.
Schedule monthly game days: deliberate fault injection sessions where the team practices detecting, diagnosing, and mitigating failures. Measure actual MTTR against the NFR target.
Conduct blameless postmortems for every SLO breach. Record the five-minute timeline of detection, diagnosis, and mitigation. Update runbooks based on what was discovered.

# nfrs/reliability-availability.yaml
sla:
  tier: standard
  availability_target_pct: 99.9
  measurement_window: rolling_30_days
  measurement_method: successful_responses_divided_by_total_requests
  success_definition:
    http_status: 2xx
    response_within_ms: 10000
    no_llm_api_error: true

error_budget:
  monthly_minutes: 43.8
  burn_rate_alerts:
    - window: 5m
      threshold_x: 14.4
      severity: page
      description: 2% error budget consumed in 1 hour
    - window: 1h
      threshold_x: 6.0
      severity: page
      description: 5% budget consumed in 6 hours
    - window: 6h
      threshold_x: 3.0
      severity: ticket
      description: 10% budget consumed in 3 days
  feature_freeze_at_pct: 50

mttr:
  target_minutes: 30
  components:
    detection_minutes: 3
    diagnosis_minutes: 10
    mitigation_minutes: 17
  tooling:
    - distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry + Jaeger)
    - on-call runbook per failure mode
    - automated rollback on health check failure

mtbf:
  target_hours: 720
  reliability: 0.9958

circuit_breaker:
  error_rate_threshold_pct: 5.0
  window_size_requests: 100
  recovery_timeout_seconds: 60
  half_open_probe_count: 3

chaos_engineering:
  cadence: monthly
  experiments:
    - name: llm_api_timeout
      description: Block all outbound calls to LLM API for 2 minutes
      expected_mttr_minutes: 5
      blast_radius: 10pct_traffic
    - name: model_rate_limit
      description: Inject 429 responses for all model calls
      expected_mttr_minutes: 3
      blast_radius: 20pct_traffic
    - name: vector_db_failure
      description: Drop all connections to the vector store
      expected_mttr_minutes: 8
      blast_radius: 10pct_traffic

A financial analysis platform deploys an agentic report-generation service. The SLA commits to 99.9% availability with a 30-second maximum P99 response time. In the first month, two incidents consumed 38 minutes of the 43.8-minute monthly error budget. The first incident was a 22-minute LLM API outage during which the circuit breaker opened and all requests failed immediately—preserving downstream system health but consuming error budget. The second was a 16-minute delay caused by a depleted daily token quota with no automated reset.

The postmortem for the first incident revealed that the MTTR target of 30 minutes was met (actual MTTR: 22 minutes), but only because the LLM provider resolved the outage—not because of any mitigation action by the team. The runbook had no fallback model configured. The team added a secondary model provider fallback, reducing the expected MTTR for LLM API outages from 22 minutes (dependent on provider recovery) to 4 minutes (automated failover). For the token quota incident, they added burn rate monitoring that alerts at 80% daily quota consumption, giving operators 2 hours to intervene before the quota resets.

Availability is not binary—a service can be available but degraded. A system that is responding but returning error messages on 30% of requests is technically 'up' but consuming error budget at a high burn rate. Availability measurement must count failed responses as downtime, not just complete system outages. Define availability based on the success rate, not the presence of a 200 OK response.

An MTTR target of 15 minutes is meaningless if the team has no runbooks and must diagnose each incident from scratch. MTTR targets must be paired with runbooks that make 15-minute recovery achievable. Validate runbooks in game days—not production incidents—to discover gaps before they matter.

A burn rate alert based on a single window misses two important patterns. A fast window (5 minutes) catches sudden spikes but generates false positives from transient errors. A slow window (1 hour) catches sustained degradation but is too slow to prevent a rapid budget burn. Multi-window alerting—page on fast + slow window breach simultaneously—reduces false positives while maintaining sensitivity.
Chaos Engineering Without a Blast Radius Limit

Injecting a fault into 100% of production traffic is not a chaos experiment—it is an outage. Every chaos experiment must specify a blast radius (percentage of traffic or number of users affected) and a kill switch. The kill switch must be tested before the experiment, not during it.
Derive NFRs from the customer commitment.
Start with the SLA percentage, compute the error budget, and work backwards to MTTR and MTBF targets. NFRs that are not linked to customer commitments have no enforcement mechanism.
Alert on burn rate, not absolute error counts.
A burn rate alert (error rate / error budget fraction) accounts for the SLA tier and the measurement period. An absolute error count alert (more than 10 errors per minute) is not SLA-aware.
Write runbooks before they are needed.
Every known failure mode (LLM API outage, rate limit, vector DB failure) must have a runbook with step-by-step mitigation actions. Include the expected MTTR in the runbook header.
Chaos engineering validates that recovery mechanisms work. Introduce LLM API timeouts in staging monthly, measure actual MTTR, and compare against the NFR target.
Configure circuit breakers conservatively at first.

Start with a higher error rate threshold (10%) and lower the threshold as operational data accumulates. A circuit breaker set too aggressively in a new system generates false positives that erode operator trust.
Publish error budget consumption to the team.
A weekly email or dashboard showing error budget consumption (X% consumed, Y% remaining) keeps reliability visible without requiring individual incidents to surface the data.

A composite SLA for the entire agentic system obscures which components are driving budget consumption. Define and measure availability for the LLM integration, the retrieval layer, and the orchestration layer independently.
Reliability at scale introduces a compound probability challenge. In a pipeline with four sequential dependencies, each with 99.9% availability, the combined availability is:

Four 99.9% components produce a 99.6% pipeline SLA—a four-fold reduction in the error budget. This compound effect means that agentic pipelines with many LLM calls and tool dependencies must either accept a lower-than-component SLA or invest in redundancy at the component level.

Redundancy options for agentic systems include: primary/fallback model providers (A/B fallover), read replicas for the vector database, and idempotent retry on tool API failures. Each redundancy mechanism reduces the effective failure rate for that component, improving the compound pipeline SLA.
Route requests across two model providers using consistent hashing. If one provider's error rate exceeds the circuit breaker threshold, the hash ring reassigns that bucket to the healthy provider.
Design tool APIs to be safely retried. An agent that calls
twice on a database failure must not create duplicate records. Use idempotency keys on all write operations.

Define a degraded operating mode for each major dependency failure. If the vector store is unavailable, the agent falls back to keyword search. If the preferred model is unavailable, it falls back to a smaller model. Document the degraded mode's SLA separately.
Multi-window burn rate alerting rules (recorded rules for error ratio, alerting rules for burn rate thresholds). Native integration with PagerDuty and Slack.
Purpose-built SLO dashboard with error budget gauges, burn rate graphs, and remaining budget countdowns. Visualises the multi-window alert state.
Managed chaos engineering platforms. Define fault injection experiments with blast radius limits, schedules, and automatic rollback. Both integrate with Kubernetes and ECS.
Circuit breaker libraries for Python and Java respectively. PyBreaker provides CLOSED/OPEN/HALF_OPEN state machine with configurable thresholds and reset timeouts.
On-call management and incident response platform. Integrates with burn rate alerts to route notifications based on severity and time of day.
Incident management and customer communication platforms. Automate external status page updates when error budget burn rate triggers an incident.

The SLO + error budget approach provides the best balance of precision and engineering cost for production agentic systems. Uptime monitoring alone misses degraded-but-available states. Full SRE practice (with 20-50% engineering time on reliability) is appropriate only for mission-critical systems. Error rate threshold monitoring is a lighter-weight alternative for systems where the compound pipeline SLA calculation makes a full error budget approach complex to implement.

Reliability NFRs and security controls interact in several important ways. Circuit breakers that open under high error rates can be triggered by an attacker who sends malformed requests to exhaust the error threshold window. Configure circuit breakers to count only errors caused by system failures, not client errors (4xx responses), to prevent this attack vector.

Denial-of-service attacks directly consume error budget. Rate limiting at the gateway is both a performance and a reliability control: it prevents individual callers from exhausting compute and triggering SLO breaches.

Never inject faults that involve security boundaries (credential invalidation, certificate expiry) without a tested recovery procedure. Security faults are harder to recover from than infrastructure faults.

In high-reliability systems, error responses must not expose internal state. A 500 error returning a full stack trace consumes error budget and leaks implementation details. Return structured error codes with opaque identifiers that map to server-side log entries.
Error budget calculations require historical availability data. Retain availability metrics for at least 13 months to support year-over-year SLA reporting and trend analysis.

Runbooks that include database credentials, API keys, or mitigation scripts must be stored in access-controlled systems, not shared wikis. A runbook accessible to an attacker is a privilege escalation pathway.

SLA availability targets translate to concrete error budgets: 99.9% allows 43.8 minutes of monthly downtime, which must be managed as a finite resource shared across all incidents and risky deployments.
Error budget burn rate alerts (not absolute error counts) are the correct alerting primitive because they account for the SLA tier and remaining budget in the current period.
MTTR targets drive investment in detection tooling, runbooks, and automated recovery; the mitigation time—not the root-cause resolution time—is what stops error budget consumption during an incident.

In multi-stage agentic pipelines, compound availability is the product of component availabilities; four 99.9% components produce only 99.6% pipeline availability, requiring redundancy at the component level.
Circuit breakers should be configured on error rate, not error count, and should exclude client errors (4xx) from the threshold window to prevent adversarial triggering.
Chaos engineering experiments must have a defined blast radius, a tested kill switch, and an expected MTTR; game days validate runbook completeness before production incidents expose gaps.
Blameless postmortems that record the five-minute recovery timeline are the primary mechanism for improving MTTR over time.
